coredata.dc:title: In-vitro evaluation of the 5-fluorouracil loaded GQDs@Bio-MOF capped with starch biopolymer for improved colon-specific delivery
coredata.dc:creator: Hassanpouraghdam, Yashar
Pooresmaeil, Malihe
Namazi, Hassan
coredata.dc:description: Herein, for the first time, the photoluminescent graphene quantum dots@Bio-metal organic framework (GQDs@Bio-MOF) nanohybrid was prepared. BET analysis obtained the average pore diameter of GQDs@Bio-MOF about 11.97 nm. The existence of nanoscale porosity in GQDs@Bio-MOF displays its suitability for 5-Fu loading owing to the smaller size of 5-Fu. 5-Fu entrapment efficiency and loading capacity were found to be ~42.04 % and ~4.20 %, respectively (5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF). The 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF was capped with starch biopolymer (St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF), fabricated sample displayed 4.67 for pHPZC. SEM analysis displayed that the St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres have a spherical shape with a diameter of ~2 μm. The in vitro drug release assay displayed better release behavior for St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF than 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF, releasing about 62.3 % of the entrapped 5-Fu within 96 h of incubation. The 5-Fu release showed the best fitting with the Higuchi model with R2 0.9884. The in vitro cytotoxicity screening outcomes displayed that the St@GQDs@Bio-MOF is a promising biocompatible carrier, with cell viability of higher than 84 %. Accumulation of the results revealed that the St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF is a new system with advantages of sustained drug release and biocompatibility that are the main criteria for each newly designed anticancer drug carrier.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Starch
Graphene quantum dots
Bio-metal-organic framework
Sustained release
Colorectal cancer
5-Fluorouracil delivery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of point of zero charge (pHpzc) for St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres
In vitro pharmacokinetics study of released 5-Fu
Obtaining the pHpzc of St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres
In vitro cytotoxicity and anticancer activity

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: University of Tabriz and Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors gratefully acknowledge the University of Tabriz and Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology , Biomedicine Institute, the Tabriz University of Medical Science for their financial supports of this research.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Proposed schematic pathway for the preparation of the St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres.
FT-IR spectra of GQDs, Bio-MOF, and GQDs@Bio-MOF (a) and GQDs@Bio-MOF, 5-Fu, 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF, starch, and St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres (b).

XRD pattern of GQDs, GQDs@Bio-MOF, starch microspheres, and St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres (a), and comparative PL emission spectra of GQDs, GQDs@Bio-MOF, and St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres (λex = 365nm).

BET-plot (a), adsorption/desorption (b), and BJH-plot of GQDs@Bio-MOF (c) for GQDs@Bio-MOF and also the BET-plot (d), adsorption/desorption (e), and BJH-plot of GQDs@Bio-MOF (f) of St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres.
SEM photographs showing the surface morphology of GQDs (a), Bio-MOF (b), GQDs@Bio-MOF (c), 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF (d), starch microspheres (e), and St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres (f).
EDX spectra of GQDs@Bio-MOF (a), 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF (b), St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres (c), and corresponding EDX mapping of St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres (d).

pHpzc of St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres (a), the effect of time and pH on swelling of St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres (b), biodegradability pattern of St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres in the presence of lysozyme at 37 °C (c) and comparison the 5-Fu release pattern from St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF and 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF (d).
5-Fu release kinetics graphs Zero order (a), First order (b), Higuchi (c), and Korsmeyer-Peppas (d) models.

Cytotoxic effect of different concentrations of the St@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres and GQDs@Bio-MOF to MCF 10 cell line (a) and also the 5-Fu, 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF, and St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF to HT 29 cell line for 48 h incubation Data represent mean ± SD (n = 3).
Comparison of 5-Fu loading percentage of different reported carriers in the literature with the current study.

head.ce:title: In-vitro evaluation of the 5-fluorouracil loaded GQDs@Bio-MOF capped with starch biopolymer for improved colon-specific delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nano-Biopolymers, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nano-Biopolymers

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nano-Biopolymers, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology, Biomedicine Institute, Tabriz University of Medical Science, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology, Biomedicine Institute, Tabriz University of Medical Science, Tabriz, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nano-Biopolymers, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nano-Biopolymers

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, for the first time, the photoluminescent graphene quantum dots@Bio-metal organic framework (GQDs@Bio-MOF) nanohybrid was prepared. BET analysis obtained the average pore diameter of GQDs@Bio-MOF about 11.97 nm. The existence of nanoscale porosity in GQDs@Bio-MOF displays its suitability for 5-Fu loading owing to the smaller size of 5-Fu. 5-Fu entrapment efficiency and loading capacity were found to be ~42.04 % and ~4.20 %, respectively (5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF). The 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF was capped with starch biopolymer (St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF), fabricated sample displayed 4.67 for pHPZC. SEM analysis displayed that the St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres have a spherical shape with a diameter of ~2 μm. The in vitro drug release assay displayed better release behavior for St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF than 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF, releasing about 62.3 % of the entrapped 5-Fu within 96 h of incubation. The 5-Fu release showed the best fitting with the Higuchi model with R2 0.9884. The in vitro cytotoxicity screening outcomes displayed that the St@GQDs@Bio-MOF is a promising biocompatible carrier, with cell viability of higher than 84 %. Accumulation of the results revealed that the St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF is a new system with advantages of sustained drug release and biocompatibility that are the main criteria for each newly designed anticancer drug carrier.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Today the occurrences of cancers, especially colorectal cancer speedily have increased, so this illness is one of the main leading reasons of death all over the world [1–4]. Colon cancer signifies cancerous growth in the colon, rectum, or cecum [5,6]. 5-Fu is the widely used chemotherapy drug for advanced and early colorectal cancer treatment. Interference with nucleoside metabolism in DNA and RNA are responsible for the observed cell apoptosis as a result of 5-Fu administration [7–9]. However, some of its side effects including severe bone marrow disorders, gastrointestinal problems, and short half-life have restricted its clinical application [10]. One effective solution to resolve these limitations is designing new effective drug delivery systems which can deliver the 5-Fu in a controlled manner [11–13]. Local treatment is one of the colon cancer therapy ways which can increase the effective dose of 5-Fu at the site of resection [14–16]. Various systems have been proposed and evaluated as drug anticancer drug carriers [17–22]. Among them, the structurally stable GQDs have attracted outstanding interest in the medicinal research area because of their distinctive properties. GQDs have some advantages such as ultra-small size, notable chemical properties, excellent aqueous solubility, non-toxicity, and photoluminescence nature [23–26]. Importantly, GQDs can provide the higher delocalized π electrons which are favorable to provide π-π interactions with drug molecules [27]. Despite these, the application of pristine GQDs hinders because they tend to aggregate. Hence the hybridization of GQDs with other nanomaterials has drawn the attention of researchers to address this concern. Among all modifying materials, MOFs are newly introduced materials that can play a vital role in improving the aggregation problem of GQDs and obtain an ideal nanohybrid. As well as the synergistic effects of GQDs and MOFs may achieve to produce a high-performance drug loading platform [28,29]. MOFs are of great interest of porous coordination polymers consisting of metal ions connected by bridging ligands. In comparison with the traditional drug carriers, MOFs have noticeable advantages like high drug loading capacity, modifiable pores, and diverse compositions [30,31]. Different types of bridging inorganic ligands were evaluated in the MOFs synthesis. The prepared MOFs from biomaterial-based inorganic ligands like amino acids are named Bio-MOFs and are more suitable for drug delivery applications [32]. In fact amino acids could be as ultimate enantiopure linkers for the MOFs synthesis due to their more availability, inexpensive, and also non-toxic nature [33]. Considering the positive features of the GQDs and Bio-MOFs in drug delivery, their hybrid can be more efficient for drug loading [34,35]. On the other hand, the encapsulation of drug/nanohybrid products with biopolymers can obtain a sustained release pattern with good biocompatibility [4,36–38]. Today there is growing attention to the encapsulation of drug/nanohybrid with natural polymers to improve their medical applications. In particular, starch has been broadly considered as an efficent colon drug carrier owing to its biodegradability, non-toxicity, renewability, excellent storage stability, and cost-effectiveness features [39–41]. This polysaccharide which abundantly can be found in the form of granules in the leaves, tubers, and seeds of many vegetables is able to preserve the bioactive substances in spherical microparticles form [42–44]. Different ways have been evaluated for starch microsphere preparation. Recently the water-in-water (w/w) emulsion method has attracted much attention because it avoids the use of toxic organic solvents and emulsifiers [39]. In fact it is reported that the starch microspheres can efficiently protect the entrapped bioactive agents from environmental degradation such as temperature, pH, humidity, microorganisms, and enzymes [43,45]. This combination makes starch as an interesting biomaterial for the development of new drug delivery systems that specifically can deliver the anticancer drug to the colon. So, recently T.Koev et al. prepared the new targeted colonic drug delivery vehicles as the drug-loaded starch based hydrogels via incorporating vanillin, 5-Fu, and doxorubicin (DOX) prior to gelatinization [46]. KeJiang et al. reported a new easy and green synthesized anionic MOF, ZJU-64-NSN for procainamide orad delivery [47]. ReKo et al. presented the glutathione-responsive PEGylated GQD-based nanosystems for diagnosis and also breast cancer treatment via DOX delivery [48]. Despite these reports, our survey of the published works displays that up to this day there was no report for the controlled delivery of the 5-Fu relies on the potential of all three starch, GQDs, and Bio-MOF. Therefore, the objective of the current research work was to synthesize and characterize the new starch-based formulation containing 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF with a sustained 5-Fu release pattern for efficient colon cancer treatment. In fact, this research work is the first report for the sustained colon delivery of 5-Fu with St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF. The success in the synthesis of St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF was approved via doing some common characterization techniques. The in vitro cytotoxicity, pH-dependent release behavior, and release mechanism were also assessed. Results showed that the fabricated St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF has huge potential for application in colon drug delivery.

Citric acid (CA), potassium bromide (KBr), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), phenylalanine, triethylamine (TEA), cadmium nitrate hexahydrate (Cd(NO3).6H2O), ethanol (EtOH), diethyl ether, soluble starch, polyethylene glycol (PEG) 20000, and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from Merck. Trisodium trimetaphosphate (TSTP) and 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazole-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) were from Sigma Aldrich. Penicillin-streptomycin, fetal bovine serum albumin (FBS), and Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) were provided from Gibco, USA. 5-Fu (vial 50 mg/5 mL) was received from Intas Pharmaceuticals, India. HT 29 a human colorectal adenocarcinoma cell line and also the MCF 10A a normal cell line were supplied by the Iranian National Cell Bank (Pasteur Institute, Tehran, Iran). Bi-distilled water was used overall in the present research work.

The successful formation of the samples was studied by doing the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (Bruker Instruments, model Aquinox 55, Germany) as KBr pellets. These spectra were recorded at room temperature and a resolution of 4 cm−1 in a range from 4000 to 400 cm−1. The pH of the solutions was assessed by a PHS-3CpH meter. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using a Siemens diffractometer with a scanning rate of 2°/min at a 2θ wavelength range from 5° to 70°. The morphology and elemental details of the samples were analyzed using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, LEO 1430VP) to obtain the energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrum, EDX map, and SEM images. For SEM analysis the dried samples were covered with a thin layer of gold and then the images were obtained. The porosity of the microspheres was evaluated by N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms at 77 K using a Sorptometer (BELsorp, Japan). Photoluminescence (PL) studies were conducted with a Jasco FP-8300 spectrofluorimeter (Japan). The concentrations of 5-Fu were monitored by using an ultraviolet-visible Shimadzu 1700 spectrophotometer (UV–Vis, Japan). Zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) tests were done employing the DLS-ZP/Particle Sizer (Microtrac, model Nanotrac Wave).

The GQDs were synthesized according to the developed method by Masteri-Farahani et al. [49] with some modifications. In summary, 2 g of CA was poured into a ceramic crucible and then heated to 200 °C in a muffle furnace. About 30 min later, the obtained orange liquid was added to the NaOH aqueous solution (10 mg/mL, 100 mL) under vigorous stirring at room temperature to be neutralized. Lastly, the solvent of the resulting GQDs aqueous solution was removed by rotary evaporation. The product was vacuum dried to yield GQDs solids and then the product was kept for additional use in the dark vial.

The Bio-MOF was fabricated in the presence of the as-synthesized GQDs according to the previously reported literature procedure [32] with some modifications. First, phenylalanine (0.34 g, 2.06 mmol) was added to a round bottom flask containing 20 mL of TEA aqueous solution (0.05 mol/L). The mixture was stirred to ensure the complete dissolution of the precursor. Subsequently, the GQDs (0.132 g) were dispersed in the reaction solution. The reaction balloon was properly sealed and the stirring was continued overnight at ambient temperature. Afterward, 10 mL of Cd(NO3).6H2O aqueous solution (0.32 g, 0.1 mmol) was added dropwise to the reaction medium and then the mixture was stirred for another 6 h. The final product was isolated by centrifugation and it was washed with water, ethanol, and diethyl ether. The collected pure solid was dried at 50 °C under reduced pressure. The same process was also applied to synthesize the pristine Bio-MOF, except no GQDs were added to the reaction solution.

For 5-Fu loading on the GQDs@Bio-MOF, about 200 mg of GQDs@Bio-MOF was immersed into a 5-Fu solution (10 mL, 1000 mg/L). The mixture was shaken for 3 days on a shaker in a dark condition and then centrifuged. The 5-Fu-loaded solid (5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF) was rinsed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS). The relevant 5-Fu content of the supernatant solution was measured employing UV–Vis spectrophotometry at a wavelength of maximum absorbance (λmax) of 5-Fu. In the following, the content of the free 5-Fu was determined considering the absorbance of the supernatant solution, the concentration-dependent absorbance standard curve of 5-Fu, and the volume of the release media. Eventually, the entrapment efficiency (Eq. (1)) was evaluated.
(1)
Drug entrapment efficiency
% w
=
Total amount of
5 − Fu
−
Free amount of
5 − Fu
Mass of drug
fed
initially
× 100
(2)
Drug loading capacity
% w
=
Mass of
5 − Fu
in carrier
Mass of carrier
× 100

To synthesize the 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF wrapped with starch microspheres, at first, 5-Fu/GQDs/MOF (0.576 g, 20 wt% with respect to starch) were dispersed into 4 mL of water employing an ultrasonic bath for 30 min. Then the starch (2.88 g) was dissolved under stirring in 14 mL of an aqueous solution of NaOH (0.36 g, 2 % w/v) containing TSTP (0.72 g, 4 % w/v). These two solutions were mixed and a mixture of 5-Fu/GQDs/MOF dispersed in 16 % w/v of starch was obtained. On the other hand, the 90 mL of PEG solution (39.6 g, 44 % w/v) was prepared by dissolving it in water. The starch-based mixture was transferred into a syringe and thereafter the syringe was cited on top of the beaker containing PEG solution at a distance of ~15 cm. The mixture was poured slowly into a beaker containing PEG solution, the temperature was kept at 30 °C using a water bath to obtain the water-in-water emulsion. Afterward, the beaker was incubated without stirring at 30 °C for 9 h. Approximately the emulsion was diluted with 100 mL of deionized water, and the resulting microspheres were separated by centrifugation and washed with water and absolute ethanol three times. Lastly, the microspheres were vacuum dried at 60 °C and preserved in dark containers for further use. The neat starch microsphere and St@GQDs@Bio-MOF were also prepared in the same way, respectively without the addition of 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF and with the addition of GQDs@Bio-MOF instead of 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF [39].

For determination of the QY of St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres, the sample was dispersed in quinine sulfate solution which was prepared from its dissolving in sulfuric acid (1 M) and then their PL was recorded. Quinine sulfate with QY = 0.54 was supposed as a reference and the QY of St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres was measured relative to it. In the end, Eq. (2) obtained the QY of the St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres.
(3)
QY S
=
QY R
×
Sample emission intensity
Reference emission intensity
×
Absorbanc of reference Absorbanc of sample
×
ŋ S 2
ŋ R 2
In Eq. (3) the ŋ parameter indicates the refractive index of the used solvent. As well as the S and R signs respectively denote the sample and reference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of point of zero charge (pHpzc) for St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres
ce:section.ce:para: pHPZC of St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres was obtained by studying the changes in pH of the aqueous solution due to the addition of the St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres. Typically, at first, a series of aqueous solutions in the pH range of 2–8 was prepared via adjusting the pH with HCl or NaOH (pH0). Then, the St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres were added to each solution. The suspension was shaken for 24 h and then the final pH of the solutions was obtained and reported as pHf. The curve of pH0-pHf (∆pH) was achieved versus pH0. Lastly, the pHPZC of St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres was identified from the point of intersection of the curve at which ∆pH = 0.

To analyze the water absorption capacity of the St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres, the specific weight of each sample was immersed in buffer solutions with two different pHs (pH 7.4 and pH 1.2). After equilibration, the mixture was separated by centrifugation and the mass of the absorbed buffer was determined based on the mass of the used buffer to prepare the suspension and the supernatant mass. Finally, the swelling percentage was calculated by the following equation:
(4)
Swelling
%
=
Wi
w − Wsup
w
Ws
× 100
Where Ws, Wi,w, and Wsup,w respectively are the weight of dry microspheres, the weight of the used buffer to make the suspension, and the weight of the supernatant [50].

To study the in-vitro biodegradation behavior of St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres, about 50 mg of the sample was suspended in 10 mL of lysozyme (1.5 μg/mL, PBS (pH 7.4). Then the samples were shaken on a shaker at 37 °C. At each selected time, the supernatant was eliminated by employing centrifugation, the residue was washed with distilled water, dried to a constant weight, and weighed. Finally, the mass loss was estimated by Eq. (5) in which, the Wt and W0 respectively are the remaining weight of each tube having a degraded sample at time t and the initial weight of each tube having a sample. The experiment was accomplished in triplicate.
(5)
Mass loss
%
=
W 0 − Wt
W 0
× 100

The release behavior of 5-Fu from 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF and St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres was explored at 37 °C by using a buffer solution with pH 7.4 which simulates the pH of colon conditions [51]. In a typical process, samples (30 mg) separately were added to the buffer solution, and the mixtures were shaken in dark. At a certain time, about 2 mL of the supernatant was taken out and replaced with an equivalent volume of corresponding fresh buffer. The absorbances of the solution were investigated at 266 nm. Finally, the release rate was calculated using Eq. (6) and the cumulative release profiles were plotted versus release time.
(6)
5 − Fu
release
% w
=
Amount of released
5 − Fu
Amount of loaded
5 − Fu
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro pharmacokinetics study of released 5-Fu
ce:section.ce:para:
The in vitro 5-Fu release data were fitted with zero-order (Eq. (7)), first-order (Eq. (8)), Higuchi (Eq. (9)), and Korsmeyer-Peppas (Eq. (10)) kinetic models [52–54] to evaluate and specify the kinetics and mechanism that control the 5-Fu releases. In this regard, the achieved correlation coefficient (R2) value for each equation shows the agreement of the obtained results with it. Likewise, the calculated value for the diffusional exponent “n” revealed the mechanism that the drug releases follow it. Accordingly, “n” is 0.45–1.0 for the non-Fickian mechanisms, is 1.0 for a case II transport, and is ≤0.45 for Fickian diffusion.
(7)
Q =
k 0
t
(8)
LnQ = Ln
Q 0
−
k 1
t
(9)
Q =
k H
t
1 2
(10)
LnQ = nLnt + Ln
k p

In the Ee. 7–10 the k0, k1, kH, and kP respectively display the rate constant of the Zero-order, First-order, Higuchi, and Korsmeyer-Peppas kinetic models. Furthermore, the Q is the amount of released 5-Fu.

The cytotoxicity of the samples; 5-Fu, 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF, and St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres was assessed via MTT assay against HT 29 cells (Fig. 9b). First, HT 29 cells with a density of 1.0 × 104 cells per well were cultivated on 96-well plates. The cultures were incubated overnight in an incubator with a standard atmosphere of 5 % CO2 at 37 °C. The test was followed by the addition of the different concentrations of the samples. After incubation for 48 h, the cultured medium was drained and about 200 μL of MTT solution (5.0 mg/mL) was added to each well. The unused MTT was removed after 4 h incubation and then the plates were rinsed with PBS. 200 μL of DMSO was added to each well and the plate was shaken for 15 min to dissolve the formed formazan crystals. The optical density was measured at 570 nm by an ELISA plate reader with a reference wavelength of 630 nm. Finally, the relative cell viability (%) was measured in comparison with the control and as absorbance values of the treated cells divided by absorbance values of untreated cells. MTT test was also performed against MCF 10A cells for GQDs@Bio-MOF and St@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres to obtain and compare their biocompatibility against normal cell lines (Fig. 9a).

Today, the design and development of a new more efficient multifunctional pH-controlled anticancer drug delivery system have remained challenging. Therefore, in this work, at first, GQDs were prepared from the CA pyrolysis. Then a combination of GQDs@Bio-MOF with nanoporous structure and the photoluminescent feature was prepared in a simple way, employing phenylalanine as a biocompatible linker with an aromatic ring in the structure that is suitable for aromatic drug loading via π–π stacking interaction. Good stability is the main advantage of the Cd-based MOFs [32,55]. Then the 5-Fu was loaded on the fabricated nanosystem. Afterward, because the main aim of this study was to fabricate and characterize a new system with sustained delivery of 5-Fu to the colon, starch microparticles coated the 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF which are believed to have trial functions of GQDs, Bio-MOF, and starch were fabricated. The successful synthesis of St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres was monitored by doing the FT-IR, XRD, SEM, and EDX analyses. The schematic route of the St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres preparation is depicted in Fig. 1 . The swelling, drug release, and biocompatibility were also investigated in detail.

To evaluate the success in the synthesis of GQDs@Bio-MOF, 5-Fu loading on it, and then coating with starch, the FT-IR measurements were carried out as shown in Fig. 2 . In the FT-IR spectrum of the GQDs, the characteristic absorption peaks at 3248 cm−1, 3031 cm−1, 1394 cm−1, and 1122 cm−1 respectively are due to the stretching vibration of the -OH, aromatic -CH groups, symmetric stretching vibrations of -COO−, and stretching vibration of CO. Furthermore, the absorption peaks due to CC stretching vibration and asymmetric stretching vibration of –COO− which merged together were seen at 1600–1800 cm−1. The pristine Bio-MOF showed the peaks related to -NH and -OH groups stretching vibration, CH stretching of methyl and ethyl groups, benzene skeleton, carbonyl stretching vibration of symmetric and asymmetric carboxylate groups, NH bending vibration, and coordinated metal stretching vibration respectively at 3256–3352 cm−1, 2864–3062 cm−1, 1588 cm−1, 1558–1401 cm−1, 1457 cm−1, and 559 cm−1. Detection of the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration of the –COO− functional groups can be a signal for the cadmium coordination to the carboxylate groups and therefore the Bio-MOF successful synthesis. The FT-IR spectrum of GQDs@Bio-MOF is relatively similar to the GQDs but when the Bio-MOF was synthesized in the presence of GQDs, the characteristic peaks of the Bio-MOF in the region of 3000–4000 cm−1 and 400–1000 cm−1 were also detected. So, simultaneous identification of the main peaks of GQDs and Bio-MOF can depict the formation of GQDs@Bio-MOF. The 5-Fu showed the absorption peaks at 3457 cm−1, 1648 cm−1, 1412 cm−1, 1221 cm−1, and below 1000 cm−1 of its FT-IR spectrum which respectively are ascribed to the presence of amine functional groups, overlapped (CN, CC, and CO) signals, the multifunctional pyrimidine ring, CF peaks, and other ones associated with the bending modes [56]. The loading of 5-Fu on GQDs@Bio-MOF was approved by comparing the FT-IR spectrum of 5-Fu with those of GQDs@Bio-MOF. 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF showed some distinctive peaks of 5-Fu with a partial shift in the wavenumber region, which provided good evidence for the existence of 5-Fu in the 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF structure. The detected shifts can be related to the formation of some interactions between the 5-Fu and GQDs@Bio-MOF. The FT-IR spectrum of starch showed a significant wide peak at 3435cm−1, which is belonged to the stretching vibration of -OH functional groups. The peaks related to CH stretching vibration, -OH bending vibration, CO, and C-O-C stretching vibration also appeared at 2926 cm−1, 2855 cm−1, 1646 cm−1, 1159 cm−1, and 1081cm−1. After covering 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF with starch biopolymer the distinctive absorption peaks of starch appeared at 1646 cm−1 and 1081 cm−1, which respectively correspond to the bending modes of -OH groups and C-O-C stretching vibration. Observation of these characteristic peaks in addition to the main absorption peaks of 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF is proof of the St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF successful synthesis. The main peaks related to 5-Fu were labeled with pale purple color in the spectrum of 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF and St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF.

XRD technique was also employed to further characterize the crystalline structure and structural parameters of the GQDs, GQDs@Bio-MOF, starch, and St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres (Fig. 3a). GQDs displayed the main broad peak at 2θ 24° of its XRD spectrum, as can be seen in Fig. 3. The observed peak could be assigned to its hexagonal lattice structure with (002) lattice spacing. The interlayer spacing of GQDs was calculated at about 3.7 Å, employing the Bragg equation. The obtained value is similar to the reported value for the graphitic materials and can approve the successful pyrolysis of CA and GQDs formation from it. Encouragingly, the crystallinity of the GQDs@Bio-MOF is confirmed by observation of the sharp diffraction peaks appearing in 2θ 16–49°. The XRD spectrum had also the main characteristic peaks of GQDs with very weak intensity, possibly because the content of GQDs is lower than that of the Bio-MOF. A wide peak similar to those of amorphous materials was detected in XRD patterns of neat starch microspheres which specify that the crosslinked starch microspheres have an amorphous structure. After wrapping the 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF with starch biopolymer, only one wide peak appeared at 2θ 14–29°, suggesting the amorphous nature of St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres as a result of the cross-linking. This observation could be a strong proof of the successful preparation of St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres. Moreover, the lower intensity of the GQDs@Bio-MOF characteristic peaks in this spectrum might be related to its good dispersion in the starch network, as well as its low percentage in comparison with starch content. The absence of any additional peaks indicates the purity of the samples [57]. According to these results, it could be concluded that the Bio-MOF was successfully synthesized in the presence of GQDs, and after drug loading, it was coated with starch in microsphere form.

The PL spectra of the GQDs, GQDs@Bio-MOF, and St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres were acquired at 365 nm of excitation wavelength to evaluate and approve their photoluminescent property. The obtained spectra ranging from 380 nm to 660 nm are shown in Fig. 3b. The PL spectrum of the luminescent GQDs revealed an emission peak occurring at λ ~ 460 nm, which shows its capability in producing blue luminescence. The PL changes along with GQDs functionalization are noticeable. So, the GQDs@Bio-MOF, and St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres display their PL emission peak respectively centered at 439 nm and 435 nm with blue-shifted emission. Additionally, in comparison with GQDs, a lower PL intensity was observed for both GQDs@Bio-MOF and St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres in the studied region. These might be due to the charge transfer, inner photoluminescence reabsorption, filter effect, and partially quenching which are in good agreement with the previous report on the introduction of MOFs and polymers into GQDs structure [25,34,58]. The QY of the St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres dispersion was determined at about 53.9 % considering the quinine sulfate as reference.

The surface area, pore volume, and mean pore diameter of the GQDs@Bio-MOF and St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres were examined by employing the BET analysis at 77 K to explore GQDs@Bio-MOF functionality for loading of 5-Fu on it. As it is displayed in Fig. 4a, the adsorption-desorption isotherm analysis obtained the average pore diameter of GQDs@Bio-MOF as about 11.97 nm, which indicates the presence of porosity in the nanoscale size and its suitability it for 5-Fu loading, due to the smaller size of 5-Fu. Additionally, the BET surface (SBET) and total pore volume (Vp) were obtained 21.45 m2 g−1 and 4.93 cm3 g−1, respectively. The results give assurance that the GQDs@Bio-MOF can be used for drug loading via its adsorption on the surface of GQDs@Bio-MOF beside the entrapment within its pores. However, in the case of the St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres (Fig. 4b) respectively the average pore diameter, SBET, and Vp were 4.84 nm, 10.24 m2 g−1, and 0.02 cm3 g−1 which are lower than the related value for the GQDs@Bio-MOF. The change in textural parameters proves the successful loading of the 5-Fu on GQDs@Bio-MOF and then the coating of 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF with starch biopolymer [59].

The particle size and morphology of the as-prepared GQDs, Bio-MOF, GQDs@Bio-MOF, 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF, and St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres were studied by SEM and the obtained images are presented Fig. 5 . SEM micrograph of the synthesized GQDs showed a uniform spherical shape with an average size of 5 ± 1 nm in diameter which reveals their nanoscale size. Bio-MOF has a regular cubic shape morphology with a diameter of about 100nm. A comparison of the neat Bio-MOF and GQDs@Bio-MOF images displays that the architectural structure of Bio-MOF is partially preserved after exposure GQDs at the nanohybrid, which is also confirmed by the XRD patterns. More investigation of the SEM image interestingly showed the incorporation of GQDs in the Bio-MOF structure has changed its external morphology, so the GQDs are spontaneously distributed on Bio-MOF during the growth process as displayed in Fig. 5c. In this Fig, the red arrows represent that the GQDs were dispersed on the surface of the Bio-MOF. The SEM image of GQDs@Bio-MOF after 5-Fu loading was also obtained to monitor the morphology changes due to drug loading. Fig. 5d showed that there is some irregular domain after drug loading which is evident for possible interaction between 5-Fu and GQDs@Bio-MOF as a result of 5-Fu loading on it. The prepared neat starch microspheres were found spherical with a relatively smooth surface. However, compared with pure starch microsphere, St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres revealed a wrinkled and rough surface with many embossing as evidenced which is beneficial to drug-releasing and proliferation required for biomedicinal applications.

EDX analysis was also performed to investigate the elemental composition of the neat GQDs@Bio-MOF and changes in its elemental composition after 5-Fu loading and encapsulation with starch biopolymer. The obtained spectrum at each step was shown in Fig. 6 . Besides the main elements of GQDs (C and O) [60], the obtained EDX spectrum for GQDs@Bio-MOF contains two new elements, including N and Cd elements (Fig. 6a), thus approving the successful synthesis of the Bio-MOF in the presence of GQDs. EDX spectrum of 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF confirmed the presence of fluorine (F), resulting from 5-Fu loading on it. EDX spectrum of St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres further confirmed the presence of P, C, O, F, and N as shown in Fig. 6c. The appearance of the P element peak can approve occurring of the crosslinking procedure. Finally, the EDX map of St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres was obtained to examine how the dispersion of 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF in microsphere structure. In the obtained EDX map the Cd element has been uniformly dispersed into the structure which is a sign of the 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF good dispersion in the composition of St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres. All these results and the absence of any extra impurity peaks confirm the successful synthesis of GQDs@Bio-MOF, 5-Fu loading on it, and then coating with starch which is consistent with the results of XRD [61].

Zeta potential analysis was performed to determine the surface charges of the samples. The aqueous dispersion of GQDs obtained surface charges of ~ −18.2 mV [62]. The observed negative surface charges of GQDs can be related to the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups on them. However, Bio-MOF presented surface charges of ~ +9.4 mV. After growth of the Bio-MOF in the presence of GQDs and also, 5-Fu loading and coating with starch the zeta potentials were obtained at about +2.5 mV and − 7.2 mV respectively. A decrease in the obtained values can be related to the introduction of the GQDs in the GQDs@Bio-MOF structure and also coating with starch biopolymer. In the following, the hydrodynamic diameter was evaluated via the DLS technique to assess the success of the samples. The hydrodynamic size distribution of GQDs was reported in the range of 4–6 nm [63]. Hoveower, the hydrodynamic diameter of the Bio-MOF, GQDs@Bio-MOF, and St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres respectively were obtained as 275 nm, 297 nm, and 4310 nm (Fig. S1). The detected increase in the size can be a sign of the MOFs successful synthesis in the presence of GQDs and then the hybrid coating with starch biopolymer which approve its capability as a colon drug carrier.

Zeta potential analysis was performed to determine the surface charges of the samples. The aqueous dispersion of GQDs obtained surface charges of ~ −18.2 mV [62]. The observed negative surface charges of GQDs can be related to the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups on them. However, Bio-MOF presented surface charges of ~ +9.4 mV. After growth of the Bio-MOF in the presence of GQDs and also, 5-Fu loading and coating with starch the zeta potentials were obtained at about +2.5 mV and − 7.2 mV respectively. A decrease in the obtained values can be related to the introduction of the GQDs in the GQDs@Bio-MOF structure and also coating with starch biopolymer. In the following, the hydrodynamic diameter was evaluated via the DLS technique to assess the success of the samples. The hydrodynamic size distribution of GQDs was reported in the range of 4–6 nm [63]. Hoveower, the hydrodynamic diameter of the Bio-MOF, GQDs@Bio-MOF, and St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres respectively were obtained as 275 nm, 297 nm, and 4310 nm (Fig. S1). The detected increase in the size can be a sign of the MOFs successful synthesis in the presence of GQDs and then the hybrid coating with starch biopolymer which approve its capability as a colon drug carrier.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Obtaining the pHpzc of St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres
ce:section.ce:para: Commonly the influence of pH on the drug release behavior of each newly designed drug carrier can be affected by its surface charge. Considering this, in current research, the pHPZC of St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres was studied to explore its potential for colon-specific drug delivery (Fig. 7a). The pHPZC of St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres was obtained at about 4.67. At a lesser pH than 4.7, the net surface charge is positive but in the medium with a pH value >4.7, the net surface charge is negative. Therefore, the obtained results favor obtaining an acceptable colon drug delivery system.

The time-dependent water absorption capacity of the St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres was studied in buffer solution with two different pHs 7.4 and 1.2 to further confirm the applicability of the designed system in colon drug delivery. Fig. 7b shows the swelling capacity of the St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres after 12 h, 24 h, 48 h, and 96 h at 37 °C. Results show that the swelling increases with time increasing and 48 h is the time that the equilibrium is reached. Moreover, it can be seen that for all of the studied times the amount of absorbed buffer in pH 7.4 is higher than pH 1.2. This may be due to the deprotonation of the hydroxyl groups in the polymeric hydrogel nanocomposite at a pH of 7.4 which lead to a formation of an expanded network and highly buffer absorption [64].

The in-vitro biodegradation of St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres was performed using the lysozyme enzyme (a model of biodegradation enzymes) at different time intervals and physiological temperature. Fig. 7c shows the obtained biodegradation profile. Lysozyme is a glycoside hydrolase that can assistances the 1, 4-beta glycosidic bond scission of each polysaccharide. Therefore the observed decreases (43 %) may be related to enzymatic scission of the starch (a polysaccharide) chain and results in glucopyranose type ring of sugar. The decrease of the degradation rate by an increase in time can be attributed to a decrease in the degradation site number. Considering the obtained results, the synthesized St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres as a 5-Fu carrier are degradable in the presence of the lysozyme enzyme [65].

The percentage of 5-Fu loading on GQDs@Bio-MOF was calculated at about 42.04 % according to the drug standard curve and UV–Vis spectroscopy. The formation of the hydrogen bond, π-π interaction between the benzene ring of 5-Fu and the organic site of GQDs@Bio-MOF, electrostatic attraction, and trapping of the drug molecules in the pores of GQDs@Bio-MOFs are some of the possible mechanisms for the 5-Fu loading on GQDs@Bio-MOF [30,34,66]. In the following the 5-Fu loading percentage for GQDs@Bio-MOF is compared to some of the proposed carriers in the published literature in Table 1 . As can be seen from Table 1, the synthesized GQDs@Bio-MOF has an acceptable drug loading percentage, representing the suitability of GQDs@Bio-MOF for cancer therapy.

Because the sustained drug release pattern is essential for anticancer drug delivery and also due to the main aim of this research is to obtain a new local drug delivery system for colon cancer therapy [14–16] the 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF was coated with starch biopolymer. Considering this, the in vitro release of 5-Fu from 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF and St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres was studied at pH 7.4 and then compared. A relatively immediate release was detected for GQDs@Bio-MOF, which could be related to the drug loading on its surface. After the burst initial drug release, a lower release rate is observed, the time for the highest observed drug release from the 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF was 48 h. However, when the 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF is coated with starch biopolymer, a slower release rate is observed (Fig. 7c). A comparison approved that the 5-Fu release from St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres is in a more sustained manner than 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF, suggesting that starch would provide an effective barrier to protect the encapsulated 5-Fu from fast releases. This behavior can be explained by the entrapping of the drug molecules in the cross-linked starch network. Indeed, the cross-linked starch can form a gel-like network and 5-Fu molecules diffuse slowly owing to its swelling properties in a more sustained manner.

Furthermore, the drug release mechanism was investigated for the St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres as the optimum system by fitting the experimental release data with four general release kinetic models [70]. As can be seen in Fig. 8 , the obtained value was 0.671 for n and the highest R2 was 0.9884 for the Higuchi model. The achieved “n” value suggested a non-Fickian mechanism release, whereby the 5-Fu partially diffuses through the swollen network and partly via the gradually expanding hydrated matrix with an increasing diffusional path length.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity and anticancer activity
ce:section.ce:para: Because it is necessary to evaluate the efficiency of each newly synthesized drug carrier from the biocompatibility viewpoint. Considering this in the current work, the standard MTT assay was accomplished against the HT 29 cells to study the cytotoxic activity of 5-Fu, 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF, and St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres for 72 h (Fig. 9b). Additionally, this test was performed against MCF 10A (as a model of normal cells) for St@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres and GQDs@Bio-MOF (Fg. 9a) to further approve the I) positive role of starch in enhancing the biocompatibility of GQDs@Bio-MOF and II) safety of the St@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres for suggesting as a new drug carrier. By contrast, As shown in Fig. 9a, even at the concentration of 50 μg/mL, the cell viability of St@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres was higher than 84 %, so it could be employed as an efficient anticancer delivery system. The increase in cell viability of St@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres compared to the GQDs@Bio-MOF confirm that the coating with starch can reduce the cytotoxicity of the system. Drug-loaded samples exhibited a chemotherapeutic effect and the inhibitory effect on the growth of the HT 29 cancer cells became more obvious with the increase of the concentration, respectively 10.03 %, 32.20 %, and 55.18 % of the treated cancer cells with 5-Fu, 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF, and St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres were killed after 48 h. The biocompatible nature of the starch coat and the sustained drug release may be the possible reasons for the reported observation. As a result of the achieved MTT outcomes, the St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres are a prospective carrier for colon cancer therapy with prolonged 5-Fu release.

In summary, the present study highlights the elaborately design and fabrication of the novel St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres as a local colon cancer treatment vehicle. First, the Bio-MOF was synthesized in the presence of GQDs, highly active sites of GQDs are more appropriate positions for the coordination of different metal ions. BET specified a pore diameter of about 11.97 nm for GQDs@Bio-MOF. 5-Fu was loaded on GQDs@Bio-MOF with high drug entrapment efficiency of 42.04 %. Due to the observed fast release profile of 5-Fu from the 5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF, it was coated with starch biopolymer. Usual characterization techniques approved the successful fabrication of the St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres. SEM analysis obtained a spherical morphology for the fabricated microspheres with shrink structure which shows their applicability in drug delivery. The result of the 5-Fu release study approved that the starch coating made the system more stable to prolong the drug release time. Interestingly, the cumulative release of St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres was 63.2 % at 4 days. The 5-Fu release kinetics from St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres was in good agreement with the Higuchi model kinetic model, R2 0.9884. In-vitro, MTT results pointed out that the St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres possessed acceptable toxicity against HT 29 cancer cells, and are not toxic in drug-free form. Overall the outcomes offered an innovative and efficient strategy based on the incorporation of three portions in one system which is very interesting because of providing an efficient synergistic effect on colon cancer cells treatment and can be proposed as a potential cancer therapy vehicle.

The following is the supplementary data related to this article.
Fig. S1
Hydrodynamic diameter of Bio-MOF (a), GQDs@Bio-MOF (b), and St@5-Fu@GQDs@Bio-MOF microspheres (c) through the DLS technique.
Fig. S1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2022.08.167.

Yashar Hassanpouraghdam and Malihe Pooresmaeil carried out the experiment. Malihe Pooresmaeil wrote the manuscript with support from Hassan Namazi as the supervisor of the project. All authors discussed the results and contributed to the final manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors gratefully acknowledge the University of Tabriz and Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology, Biomedicine Institute, the Tabriz University of Medical Science for their financial supports of this research.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectrochim. Acta A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectrochim. Acta A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: [56] V.K. Rastogi, M.A. Palafox, Spectrochim Acta A Mol Biomol Spectrosc, 79 (2011) 970-977.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: J. Surf. Eng. Mater. Adv. Technol.
